data.title: FuboTV repurchases $140.2M of convertible notes due 2026
data.description:
• FuboTV (FUBO) repurchased $140.2M aggregate principal amount of its outstanding 3.25% convertible senior notes due 2026.
  • The notes were repurchased at 100% of principal plus accrued and unpaid interest, in accordance with the indenture terms.
  • The repurchased notes were originally due on February 15, 2026.
  • The transaction follows Fubo’s receipt of $145M in proceeds from a term loan tied to its 2025 business combination with Hulu + Live TV.
  • Fubo also confirmed that no repurchases were made for its Convertible Senior Notes due 2029 after a fundamental change notice to holders.
  • FUBO shares up 3% post-market.

data.title: S&P Global raises dividend by ~1%
data.description:
• S&P Global (SPGI) declared $0.97/share quarterly dividend, 1% increase from prior dividend of $0.96.
  • Forward yield 0.71%
  • Payable March 11; for shareholders of record Feb. 25; ex-div Feb. 25.
  • The 1.0% increase takes into consideration the expected spin of S&P Global's Mobility division into an independent, public company in 2026.
  • See SPGI Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: 374Water regains compliance with NASDAQ listing requirement
data.description:
• 374Water (SCWO) on Wednesday said it has regained compliance with Nasdaq’s minimum bid price requirement.
  • The company said Nasdaq notified it that it now meets Listing Rule 5550(a)(2) after its shares maintained a closing bid price of at least $1.00 for 10 consecutive trading days, achieved on January 9, 2026.
  • The exchange has closed the matter, and the stock will continue to trade on the Nasdaq Capital Market.
  • 374Water had previously been notified in January 2025 that it was out of compliance after its shares traded below $1.00 for 30 consecutive business days.
  • SCWO closed -2.32% at $2.52.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Cheniere Energy upgraded at Wolfe as 'fortress in the LNG storm'
data.description:
Cheniere Energy (LNG) +3.1% in Wednesday's trading as Wolfe Research upgraded shares to Outperform from Peer Perform with a $220 price target, citing the stock's compelling valuation even with overbuild headwinds.

Cheniere (LNG) shares have been under pressure amid concerns over an LNG overbuild from a surge of new less contracted U.S. competitor projects and Qatar, but Wolfe analyst Keith Stanley sees the recent weakness as overdone with the stock now trading at its view of floor value based on a conservative distributable cash flow assuming a step-down in contract rates as contracts roll off in 2036 and beyond.

Cheniere's (LNG) 14% free cash flow yield in 2028-29 upon the Corpus 8 & 9 startup is the highest in the sector, and is even more remarkable considering the company is 90% contracted under fixed-fee arrangements for an average of 16 years, with average A- rated credit
counterparties, the analyst said, adding the first contract expiration is not until 2036, by which time LNG could easily have entered an up-cycle.

As negative news flow around competitor project final investment decisions fades this year, Stanley believes investors will re-visit Cheniere (LNG) and see it as an attractive entry point for a high quality equity with undervalued cash flow.

data.title: Tech Voices: DeSantis vs. AI, Tesla's FSD, smart glasses market
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector. 

  • Florida Governor Ron DeSantis has called for more regulation of AI and data centers, which he believes pose a credible threat to the U.S. economy.

“This technology threatens to upend key parts of our economy in ways that can leave many Americans out of work and with consumers footing the bill for the cost of power-intensive data centers,” DeSantis said during his state-of-the-state address, according to Bloomberg. “It presents real perils for children and parents.”

DeSantis has said he is particularly concerned about the strain data centers will put on the electrical grid. 

  • Elon Musk said Tesla (TSLA) is converting its Full Self-Driving technology to a monthly subscription from a one-time fee product.

"Tesla will stop selling FSD after Feb. 14. FSD will only be available as a monthly subscription thereafter," Musk announced on X on Wednesday.

Musk didn't immediately offer a reason for the change.

  • The burgeoning smart glasses market is expected to expand to as much as $200B by 2040, according to HSBC.

Analysts for the bank based their estimate on early strong sales of smart glasses made by eyewear maker EssilorLuxottica (ESLOF) (ESLOY) and Meta (META). They added that the number of smart glasses users is expected to soar from 15M in 2025 to 289M by the end of 2039, with Apple (AAPL), Samsung (SSNLF), or Amazon (AMZN) likely to enter the market in the next couple of years, according to Bloomberg.

“The more competition, the more likely this market will gain critical mass and attract R&D efforts to the benefit of all players,” HSBC analysts said in their note, Bloomberg said.

data.title: Compounding pharmacy sues Lilly, Novo over GLP-1 drug competition
data.description:
Arizona-based Strive Compounding Pharmacy has sued Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) in a Texas federal court alleging that the drug giants coordinated to stifle competition by hindering access to compounded versions of their blockbuster GLP-1 weight loss drugs.

The two companies discouraged "providers, telehealth platforms, and patients from prescribing and accessing compounded GLP-1s," per a news release. 

Lilly manufactures Zepbound and Mounjaro (tirzepatide), while Novo makes Wegovy and Ozempic (semaglutide).

"It's a fight for the right of every patient to have access to the medicine their doctor prescribes, at a price that isn't dictated by a monopoly,"  Strive CEO Nate Hill said in a statement.

Strive's argument in the case is predicated on the idea that some patients need a personalized version of a brand-name drug as well as a law that allows compounders to make brand drugs if a shortage has been declared. The latter is currently not the case with any of the companies' GLP-1s.

Lilly previously sued Strive over its manufacture of compounded versions of its GLP-1 drugs, but a Delaware federal judge tossed the case in October 2025 over a jurisdictional issue. 

Novo has also sued compounders in the past over GLP-1 therapies.

Requests for comment from Lilly and Novo have yet to be returned to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: CIO survey shows 3.8% increase to software budgets in 2026: Morgan Stanley
data.description:
A survey of chief information officers by Morgan Stanley found enterprises are expected to increase their software budgets by 3.8% year over year in 2026, with Microsoft (MSFT) remaining the clear leader in market share.

Software budgets were expected to increase by 3.7% in the 2025 survey.

"Within that improving software backdrop, we view Microsoft as the best-positioned platform to continue benefitting from improving spending conditions, with CIOs' weighted-average growth expectations at +7.3% in 2026," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Keith Weiss, in a Wednesday investor note.

Ninety-two percent of CIOs surveyed intend to use Microsoft's generative artificial intelligence products over the next 12 months. It was 95% last year.

"Microsoft Azure again screens as CIOs' preferred public cloud vendor (and is expected to remain so over the next 3 years) with CIOs indicating 53% of application workloads reside in Microsoft Azure today, with expectations remaining durable over the next 3 years – all while the broader pace of public cloud adoption is accelerating, based on our CIO Survey," Weiss noted.

Microsoft's relationship with OpenAI also continues to play a role, as 37% of CIOs expect to utilize Azure's OpenAI services. However, this is a steep drop from the 57% that expected to use the service in the second quarter 2025 survey.

While the results show Microsoft as holding the largest market share of genAI spending by enterprises over the next three years at 34%, Amazon (AMZN) ranks second at 15%, followed by OpenAI (OPENAI) and Salesforce (CRM) with 9% each and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) with 6%. 

Morgan Stanley rates Microsoft as Overweight and a "top pick" with a $650 price target.

data.title: OpenAI signs multi-billion dollar computing deal with Cerebras
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) announced on Wednesday that it has signed a deal with Cerebras Systems (CBRS) to provide it with 750 megawatts of computing power over a few years.

The deal between the ChatGPT maker and the artificial intelligence chip startup is for three years and is worth “billions,” The Wall Street Journal reported. CNBC reported the deal is worth more than $10B. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is a personal investor in Cerebras.

“OpenAI’s compute strategy is to build a resilient portfolio that matches the right systems to the right workloads. Cerebras adds a dedicated low-latency inference solution to our platform. That means faster responses, more natural interactions, and a stronger foundation to scale real-time AI to many more people,” said Sachin Katti of OpenAI, in a statement.

“We are delighted to partner with OpenAI, bringing the world’s leading AI models to the world’s fastest AI processor. Just as broadband transformed the internet, real-time inference will transform AI, enabling entirely new ways to build and interact with AI models,” Andrew Feldman, co-founder and CEO of Cerebras, added.

The computing power will come online in multiple tranches, ending in 2028, the companies added.

Separately, on Wednesday, it was reported Cerebras was in discussions to raise $1B that would value the company at $22B.

Last month, it was reported that Cerebras, which competes against semiconductor juggernauts such as Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD), was targeting an initial public offering for the second quarter of 2026.

Cerebras was recently named by Seeking Alpha's analysts one of the more notable AI companies that could go public this year.

data.title: Penumbra gains amid takeover speculation
data.description:
• Penumbra (PEN) rose 3.8% at least partly amid some takeover speculation.
  • Penumbra (PEN) may have drawn takeover interest, according to traders, who cited a Betaville "uncooked" alert that was circulating on Wednesday. It's not known who may be circling Penumbra. 
  • Medical device maker Penumbra (PEN) has a market cap of $11.8 billion.

data.title: Mid-to-Low cap consumer discretionary stocks facing poor EPS revision grades
data.description:
As the earnings season kicks off, a review of mid to low cap consumer discretionary stocks reveals several companies currently receiving the weakest grades for earnings momentum.

This analysis examines stocks arranged according to their EPS revision grades, highlighting those at the bottom of the scale.

The focus centers on companies that have earned an ‘F’ grade for EPS revisions, indicating particularly weak earnings momentum. This grade reflects significant downward pressure on analyst expectations for future earnings.

Here are the consumer discretionary stocks with the lowest EPS revision grades:

  1. American Public Education (APEI) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  2. Arko Corp. (ARKO) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  3. Cracker Barrel Old Country Store, Inc. (CBRL) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  4. Caesars Entertainment, Inc. (CZR) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  5. Fox Factory Holding Corp. (FOXF) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  6. Six Flags Entertainment Corporation (FUN) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  7. Global Business Travel Group, Inc. (GBTG) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  8. Genesco Inc. (GCO) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  9. MarineMax, Inc. (HZO) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F
  10. Mohawk Industries, Inc. (MHK) — EPS/Revenue Revisions Grade: F

Consumer Discretionary ETFs: (XLY), (VCR), (FXD), (FDIS), (RSPD), and (RXI)
